Dataset and the questions
Portfolio composition

Section 2. A profitability problem

Aggregate loss ratio is rising
Deterioration by product line
Diagnosing COMP_N
Mispriced segments by bonus and age
Coverage lines and bodily injury

Section 3. A different kind of risk

Loss concentration in the tail
The volatility paradox
Where capital and pricing converge

Conclusion

Five actions


Section one establishes portfolio scale and composition. Section two traces the profitability story across aggregate, product, driver, and coverage dimensions. Section three examines volatility and capital risk, where the most counter intuitive findings sit. Orange marks concern throughout. Green marks acceptable territory. The dashed 70 percent line is the underwriting break even reference used consistently across all slides.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
1 · Dataset and the questions


The data

Spanish non life motor insurer, Mendeley Data
352,338 policy years after cleaning, which is 99.5 percent of raw
2022 to 2024, growing from 67K to 168K records
50 columns. 47 original plus licence_tenure, age_band, value_quintile

Two questions

Where is profitability falling?
Loss ratio equals incurred divided by premium.
Where does volatility sit?
Tail percentiles, CV, and Gini.










Figure 1









The book is large and growing fast, doubling in volume from 2022 to 2024. The two analytical questions that follow are where profitability is falling and where volatility sits.




Policy volume more than doubled from 67K in 2022 to 168K in 2024. Four variables carried missing values, each affecting under one percent of rows. Dropping those rows retains 99.5 percent of the dataset without distorting any key distribution. The loss ratio measures average profitability. The coefficient of variation and Gini coefficient measure the spread and concentration of losses beyond the average.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
2 · Portfolio composition










Figure 2






What dominates the book

CC is the workhorse product at 58 percent. COMP_E sits second at 26 percent.
Good history drivers make up the bulk of the book.
Almost everyone pays annually.
The book is mostly renewals rather than new business.





Composition is broadly stable year on year. This means the loss ratio movement examined next is a behavioural and pricing story inside the existing book, not a mix shift story.




The bar chart shows policy type share across the full three year portfolio. CC dominates at 58 percent, with COMP_E second at 26 percent. TP and COMP_N are both small. The chart shows three year aggregate shares, not a year by year breakdown. Good drivers make up the bulk of the book and almost everyone pays annually. The reason for deterioration will be further analysed in the following slides.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
5 · Aggregate loss ratio is rising










Figure 3






The headline number

66% → 75% loss ratio in two years (+9 ppt, +14% relative)
Frequency barely moves: 0.302 → 0.311
Premium per exposure falls 9%: €446 → €404

Diagnosis

Claim cost held steady. Pricing softened. The loss ratio rose because the denominator shrank, not the numerator.



This is the headline. Loss ratio moved from sixty-six percent in 2022 to seventy-five percent in 2024, a nine percentage-point lift in two years. The first instinct on seeing that is to ask whether claims got worse. The middle panel says no. Claim frequency drifted from 0.302 to 0.311, statistically inconsequential. The right panel is the telling one. Average premium per exposure fell from four hundred and forty-six euro to four hundred and four euro. The portfolio is growing volume, but it is doing so by writing each unit of exposure cheaper than it did two years ago. The aggregate loss ratio rose because the denominator shrank, not because claims accelerated. That diagnosis reframes the next several slides. We are looking for which segments the softer pricing has hit hardest, not which segments are claiming more often.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
6 · Deterioration concentrates by product










Figure 4






Trajectories diverge

COMP_N: 73% → 102% ⚠ crossed break-even
TP: 30% → 65% (doubled, still safe)
TPG: 60% → 80%
COMP_E: ~60% → 80%
CC: ~65% throughout — the only stable product

Read
Deterioration is broad-based, but COMP_N is the only product losing money.


The aggregate trend hid a structural story. Four of the five products climbed, but at very different rates and from very different starting points. COMP_N is the clear concern: it crossed the underwriting break-even line of one hundred percent in 2024, which means it is now paying out more in claims than it collects in premium. Comprehensive with excess and third-party-plus-glass climbed in step from around sixty percent into the high seventies. Third-party-only doubled from thirty to sixty-five percent, but from such a low base that it remains profitable. CC, which is the largest book at fifty-eight percent of volume, oscillated around sixty-five percent and is the only line genuinely holding its profitability. The next slide diagnoses COMP_N specifically, because crossing break-even on any product line is the kind of finding that demands a separate investigation.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
7 · Diagnosing COMP_N: frequency, not severity










Figure 5






Decomposing the loss ratio

Loss ratio ≈ frequency × severity ÷ premium


Severity sits in a narrow €750 – €1,100 band across products
Frequency varies almost 8×: 0.13 (TP) → 1.04 (COMP_N)

The mechanism
COMP_N has no excess, so policyholders claim for every small bump. Same accident severity, far more claims filed.
Remedy → reintroduce an excess or load the product


The loss-ratio formula has three components: frequency, severity, and average premium. Decomposing COMP_N against the other products isolates which one is doing the damage. Severity, on the right panel, sits in a tight band between roughly seven hundred and fifty and eleven hundred euro per claim across all five products, with COMP_N actually a touch on the lower end. So COMP_N policyholders are not having worse accidents. The frequency panel on the left is where the story lives. Frequency varies by nearly a factor of eight, from 0.13 claims per exposure on basic third-party to 1.04 on COMP_N. The mechanism is the missing excess: when policyholders carry no out-of-pocket cost for small losses, they file every one. The remedy is either reintroducing an excess to suppress nuisance claims, or loading the product to recover the cost of the higher claim count.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 8 · Mispriced segments: bonus tier and driver age
text:
8 · Mispriced segments: bonus tier and driver age










Figure 6














Figure 7









Bad history: 53% → 93% loss ratio. Neutral: peaked at 102% in 2024. 76+ drivers: 102% loss ratio while paying below portfolio average premium. The pricing model treats both risky-history tiers and senior drivers as cheaper than experience supports.




Two pricing mis-calibrations sit side by side. The left chart tracks loss ratio by bonus tier through time. In a well-calibrated book, bad-history drivers should run a low loss ratio at high premium because the loading offsets their risk. The data shows the loading is not keeping pace. Bad history climbed from fifty-three to ninety-three percent in two years; neutral briefly overshot bad history in 2024 at one hundred and two percent. Only good history stayed inside acceptable territory. The right panel adds a second mis-calibration on driver age. The eighteen-to-twenty-five band is correctly priced high, but the curve flattens through middle age and falls again at seventy-six-plus, where the loss ratio crosses break-even at one hundred and two percent. The segment is small at under one percent of the book, but the misalignment is structural and surfaces in the loadings, not the volume.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 9 · Coverage lines: liability and bodily injury
text:
9 · Coverage lines: liability and bodily injury










Figure 8






Two-thirds of premium runs hot

Liability — €64M · LR 74%
Property damage — €26M · LR 72%
The four smaller lines all sit at 40 – 52% (profitable)

Inside liability: bodily injury is the engine

~€4,940 average cost per BI claim
4× the next-largest coverage (Property damage €706)
~9% of claims by count, but anchors the LR uplift

Action → BI sub-tariff must track medical and legal inflation indicators


The product-level view tells one part of the story; the coverage-line view tells the other. Premium is concentrated in two coverage lines that together carry roughly two-thirds of total premium, and both run above the seventy-percent reference. Liability at sixty-four million euro runs a loss ratio of seventy-four percent. Property damage at twenty-six million runs seventy-two. The other four lines run comfortably profitable in the forty-to-fifty-percent band. Inside liability, the cookbook splits claims into property damage and bodily injury. A bodily injury claim costs roughly four thousand nine hundred forty euro on average, more than four times the next largest category. They are only nine percent of claims by count, but their structural cost is what pulls the liability line up. The actionable insight is that the bodily injury sub-tariff must be benchmarked against external medical and legal inflation indicators, not against the portfolio’s own claim trend.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
10 · Losses are extremely concentrated










Figure 9






Gini = 0.96
A handful of policies drive virtually all of the loss.



Slice
Share of total loss




Bottom 80% of policies
< 5%


Top 5% of policies
> 80%


Top 1% of policies
~ 50%



Why this matters

Reinsurance must be calibrated against the tail, not the mean
Uniform rate increases punish the eighty percent who barely claim, while under-loading the policies that drive losses



The Gini coefficient of 0.96 on incurred per policy is high even by motor-insurance standards. The Lorenz curve sits hard against the floor for most of its length, then climbs almost vertically at the very end. In plain terms, the bottom eighty percent of policies contribute under five percent of total losses, while the top five percent carry over eighty percent and the top one percent alone carry roughly half. Two consequences follow. First, reinsurance cover should be benchmarked against the very top of the distribution, not against an average loss, because that small tail is what creates the capital risk. Second, uniform rate increases applied across the whole book are a poor instrument. They disproportionately raise prices on the eighty percent of policies that barely claim, while still leaving the loss-driving tail under-loaded relative to its actual cost.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
11 · The volatility paradox










Figure 10






Worst loss ratio ≠ most volatile

COMP_N, with the worst loss ratio, has the lowest CV (~3.6) — high claim volume averages out
TPG / Bad history (CV 17.6) and CC / Neutral (CV 18.6) are where reserve risk actually lives

Two different problems



Problem
Tool




Profitability
Pricing, excess design


Volatility
Reinsurance, capital reserves



Recalibrating COMP_N does not fix the capital exposure on TPG and CC.


This slide carries the most counter-intuitive finding in the deck. The ranking of segments by loss ratio is not the same as the ranking by capital risk. COMP_N has the worst loss ratio, but its CV is the lowest in the matrix at roughly three point six. The reason is volume: COMP_N has so many small claims per policy that the distribution is dense and the average per-policy outcome is predictable. By contrast, third-party-plus-glass with bad history sits at a CV of 17.6, and CC with a neutral bonus tier sits at 18.6. These are the segments where the standard deviation of incurred is nearly twenty times the mean, which means any single year could deliver an outcome far above or below the long-run average. Profitability and volatility are different problems. Pricing addresses profitability. Reinsurance and capital reserves address volatility. Fixing one does not fix the other.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 12 · Where capital and pricing risk converge
text:
12 · Where capital and pricing risk converge


Figure 11



The most concerning red cells are CC / Neutral and the entire COMP_N row. Good-history CC, the workhorse of the book, is the only large segment in blue. A correction targeting only the bad-history loading would miss most of the money at risk.




This grid replaces the treemap from the main report with a simpler heatmap version. Each cell shows premium volume and loss ratio for a specific combination of policy type and bonus tier. Two patterns stand out. The COMP_N column is uniformly red across all three bonus tiers, confirming that the product structure itself drives the loss ratio, regardless of the customer segment. Within CC, the largest product, the neutral bonus cell stands out in red at eighty-seven percent, while the good-history cell is blue and represents the largest profitable region in the book. The action implication is that a rate review touching only the bad-history loading would miss the neutral tier and the COMP_N product, which together hold most of the loss-making premium. Pricing remedies need to be applied at the product-by-tier cell level, not at the level of the bonus tier alone.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
11 · Five actions


1. Liability and property damage first. These two coverage lines together hold two thirds of premium and both run above the 70 percent threshold. Any pricing review starts here.
2. Bodily injury sub tariff. Roughly 9 percent of claims by count but structurally 4 times the cost. Index against medical and legal inflation independently.
3. Restructure COMP_N. Reintroduce a customer excess or load the product by approximately 30 percent. This is the only product breaching break even.
4. Recalibrate bonus loading at the cell level. The neutral tier overshot bad history in 2024. Review by product crossed with tier, not by tier alone.
5. Reinsurance against tail concentration. Gini 0.96, top 1 percent of policies drive half of losses, CC neutral CV 18.6. Calibrate cover against the tail, not the mean.





What this avoids


A uniform rate increase would do three things:

Penalise the 80 percent of policies that contribute under 5 percent of losses.
Under load the 1 percent that drives half of total incurred.
Miss the COMP_N structural issue entirely.

Targeting is key.






The five actions follow from the evidence in priority order, matching Section 10.3 of the main report. Liability and property damage hold two thirds of premium and both exceed the threshold, so any pricing review starts there. The bodily injury sub tariff must track external inflation indicators independently. COMP_N is the only product breaching break even and the frequency decomposition points clearly to the missing excess as the mechanism. Bonus tier loadings need recalibration at the product by tier cell level, not by tier alone. Reinsurance cover should be reset against the tail concentration shown by the Gini coefficient and the HOP, not against the mean.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 3 · Loss ratio is rising because pricing softened
text:
3 · Loss ratio is rising because pricing softened










Figure 3






Why loss ratio increased

Loss ratio rose from 66 percent to 75 percent in two years. This is a 9 ppt absolute lift, or 14 percent relative.
Claim frequency barely moves at 0.302 to 0.311.
Premium per exposure falls 9 percent at €446 to €404.





Claim cost held steady while pricing softened, naturally causing the loss ratio to increase.




The loss ratio rose from 66 percent in 2022 to 75 percent in 2024, a 9 percentage point lift. The middle panel shows claim frequency moved from 0.302 to 0.311, which is negligible. The right panel is where we understand why the loss ratio increases. Average premium per exposure fell from €446 to €404, a 9 percent decline. The loss ratio rose because the premium denominator shrank, not because claims worsened. This diagnosis directs attention toward pricing calibration rather than claims management.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 4 · Only COMP_N has crossed break even
text:
4 · Only COMP_N has crossed break even










Figure 4





Which policy to take note of

COMP_N rose from 73 percent to 102 percent. This is the only product to cross break even.
TP doubled from 30 percent to 65 percent. It is still safe.
TPG rose from 60 percent to 80 percent.
COMP_E rose from approximately 60 percent to 80 percent.
CC held around 65 percent. It is the only product genuinely stable.





Deterioration is broad based, however COMP_N is the only product paying out more in claims than it collects in premium. The next slide diagnoses why.




All five products deteriorated, however the scale differs sharply. COMP_N crossed the 100 percent break even line in 2024, meaning it now pays out more in claims than it collects in premium. TPG and COMP_E climbed from around 60 percent into the high seventies. TP doubled from 30 to 65 percent but remains profitable due to its low starting point. CC held around 65 percent throughout and is the only product maintaining stability. COMP_N is the immediate priority because it is the only product currently running at a loss.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 5 · COMP_N claims volume drives the loss, not severity
text:
5 · COMP_N claims volume drives the loss, not severity










Figure 5






Decomposing the loss ratio
Loss ratio roughly equals frequency times severity divided by premium.

Severity sits in a narrow €750 to €1,100 band across products.
Frequency varies almost 8 times. This ranges from 0.13 for TP up to 1.04 for COMP_N.





The COMP_N product structure itself drives the loss. The ECDF analysis in the full report confirms the problem is a broad upward shift in mid sized claims, not catastrophic tail events.




Decomposing the loss ratio isolates whether COMP_N’s problem is larger claims or more claims. The severity panel shows all five products sit in a tight band of €750 to €1,100 per claim, with COMP_N at the lower end. The frequency panel shows the divergence. COMP_N records 1.04 claims per exposure against 0.13 for TP, an eight fold difference. The mechanism is the absence of a customer excess. With no out of pocket cost for small claims, policyholders file every minor incident. The ECDF analysis in the full report confirms the distribution shift is concentrated in mid sized claims, not catastrophic events.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 6 · Bonus tier and senior loadings are under calibrated
text:
6 · Bonus tier and senior loadings are under calibrated










Figure 6













Figure 7






The pricing model treats both risky history tiers and senior drivers as cheaper than what the data suggests. Bad history climbed from 53 percent to 93 percent in two years. The neutral tier overshot bad history in 2024 at 102 percent. Drivers aged 76 plus run at 102 percent while paying below average premium.



The loading structure across all three bonus tiers and the 76 plus age band needs review against recent claims data.




The left chart shows bad history climbed from 53 percent in 2022 to 93 percent in 2024. The neutral tier briefly overshot bad history in 2024 at 102 percent. In a well calibrated book, bad history should run a low loss ratio because its higher premium offsets elevated risk. The data shows loading have not kept pace with actual claims experience. The right charts reveals a second mis-calibration. The 76 plus age band runs a 102 percent loss ratio while paying below average premium. The segment is small at under one percent of the book, however the gap between price and risk is structural and requires correcting at the tariff level.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 7 · Bodily injury anchors the liability loss
text:
7 · Bodily injury anchors the liability loss


Figure 8
Bodily injury averages approximately €4,940 per claim, which is roughly 4 times the next largest category. Although bodily injury represents only 9 percent of claims by count, its severity drives a disproportionate share of the liability loss.



The bodily injury sub tariff must track medical and legal inflation indicators independently.




Liability at €64M is the largest coverage line and runs a 74 percent loss ratio. Property damage at €26M runs 72 percent. These two lines together carry two thirds of total premium and both sit above the profitability threshold. The four smaller lines run between 40 and 52 percent and remain profitable. Within liability, a bodily injury claim averages €4,940, more than four times the next largest category. Bodily injury accounts for only 9 percent of claims by count, however its high individual cost drives a disproportionate share of the overall liability loss ratio. The sub tariff must be tracked against medical and legal cost inflation independently of the rest of liability.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 8 · The top 1 percent of policies drive half of losses
text:
8 · The top 1 percent of policies drive half of losses










Figure 9






Gini = 0.96
A handful of policies drive virtually all of the loss.



Slice
Share of total loss




Bottom 80% of policies
< 5%


Top 5% of policies
> 80%


Top 1% of policies
~ 50%







Reinsurance must be calibrated against the tail of the distribution, not the mean. A uniform rate increase would penalise the 80 percent of policies that contribute under 5 percent of losses while under loading the 1 percent driving half of them. Targeting is key.




The Gini coefficient of 0.96 is high even by motor insurance standards. The Lorenz curve hugs the floor for most of its length before climbing almost vertically at the right end. The bottom 80 percent of policies contribute under 5 percent of total incurred. The top 5 percent carry over 80 percent, and the top 1 percent alone carry roughly half. Reinsurance must be benchmarked against the top of the distribution, not the mean, because the tail generates the capital risk. Uniform rate increases are a poor response. They penalise the 80 percent of policyholders who contribute almost nothing to losses while leaving the loss driving tail under priced relative to actual cost.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 9 · The worst loss ratio is not the most volatile
text:
9 · The worst loss ratio is not the most volatile










Figure 10






The volatility paradox
Three segments are compared on incurred per claim (log scale).

COMP_N / Good history — low CV at approximately 3.6. High claim volume averages out. The orange line barely moves.
CC / Good history — moderate spread. The orange line shifts noticeably between frames.
CC / Neutral — CV 18.6. The orange line swings widely. This is where reserve risk sits.





Profitability and volatility are different problems demanding different tools. Pricing and excess design address profitability. Reinsurance and capital reserves address volatility. The CC and TPG segments warrant a reinsurance review against actual tail risk, separate from any pricing action on COMP_N.




Three segments are plotted: COMP_N good history, CC good history, and CC neutral. Blue points are individual policy incurred values on a log scale. The orange line is the sample mean for 25 randomly drawn policies per frame. COMP_N good history shows a tight point cloud and a nearly stationary orange line, confirming low CV. CC good history shows more spread. CC neutral shows the widest point cloud and the most erratic orange line, confirming CV of 18.6. The volatility is visible in how far the orange line moves between frames. COMP_N is a profitability problem. CC neutral is a capital reserve problem. They require different responses.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
section: 10 · COMP_N and CC neutral together hold most loss making premium
text:
10 · COMP_N and CC neutral together hold most loss making premium








Figure 11







The largest red tiles are CC neutral at 87 percent and the entire COMP_N block. Good history CC, which is the workhorse of the book, is the only large tile in blue. A rate correction touching only the bad history loading would miss most of the premium actually at risk. The neutral tier and COMP_N product structure both warrant equal attention.




The treemap encodes premium volume through tile area and loss ratio through colour. Blue sits below 70 percent. Red sits above. Two patterns stand out. The COMP_N block is uniformly red across all three bonus tiers, confirming the product structure drives the loss ratio regardless of customer segment. Within CC, the neutral bonus tile is the most prominent red region at 87 percent, while the good history tile is blue and is the largest single profitable region in the book. CC with bad history at 61 percent is actually one of the more profitable segments. A rate review touching only the bad history loading would miss the neutral tier and COMP_N, which together hold most of the loss making premium.

title: My Visual Analytics Journey
text:
Latest Updates

📘 Hands-on Exercises



Hands-on Exercise 5 — Correlation Analysis, Heatmaps, Ternary Plots, Parallel Coordinates Plots, and Treemaps
Explored (1) visual correlation analysis covering scatterplot matrices, corrgrams, glyph types, and hierarchical reordering, (2) heatmaps covering data transformation, clustering, seriation, and interactive versions, (3) ternary plots covering three-part compositional data with ggtern and plotly, (4) parallel coordinates plots covering grouping, scaling, faceting, and interactive D3 versions, and (5) treemaps covering hierarchical part-of-whole data, palette and layout choices, and drill-down interactivity.
Read more →




🖥️ In-class Exercises



In-Class Exercise 5 — Visual Multivariate Analysis with Tableau
Built two treemap views to practise multivariate visual analysis. The first treemap uses Singapore private residential property transaction data across 2025, encoding transaction volume as tile area and median unit price per square foot as tile colour to expose the joint distribution of market activity and price intensity across planning areas. The second treemap uses the Superstore sample dataset, encoding total sales as tile area and total profit as tile colour to reveal where revenue volume and margin diverge across geographic markets.
Read more →




📝 Take-Home Assignments



Take-home Assignment 1 — coming soon
Independent projects applying visual analytics techniques to real-world datasets, culminating in a polished analytical deliverable.
Read more →

title: Hands-On Exercise Index
text:
Hands-on Exercise 1 — Basic Principles and Essential Components of ggplot2
Explore the Layered Grammar of Graphics and learn how ggplot2 implements it through its essential components.
Read more →





Hands-on Exercise 2 — Beyond ggplot Fundamentals
Explored several ggplot2 extensions for creating more elegant and effective statistical graphics — covering label placement, themes, and combining plots into one figure.
Read more →





Hands-on Exercise 3 — Programming Interactive Data Visualisation and Animated Statistical Graphics with R
Explored several R packages for adding interactivity to static ggplots covering hover tooltips, linked highlighting, click-throughs, and coordinated multiple views.

Explored (1) fair comparisons across units of different sizes covering funnel plots, control limits, and overdispersion adjustments, (2) visualising distributions covering ridgeline plots, raincloud plots, quantile colouring, and gradient fills along the x-axis, (3) visual statistical analysis covering one-sample tests, ANOVA, correlation tests, and chi-square tests with statistical details embedded in the chart, and (4) visualising uncertainty covering standard error bars, confidence intervals, gradient intervals, and Hypothetical Outcome Plots (HOPs).
